lines.text: Alright, alright — settle in. I've got an announcement, and no, before anyone asks, it is not a pop quiz.
lines.action: leans against the desk

lines.text: Wait, then why do I feel like it's a pop quiz.
Because you're always a little bit taking a pop quiz, Jordan. It's a personality thing.

Here's the thing. Every year I get forty essays about 'what matters to you,' and forty kids lie about it in slightly different fonts. This year we're doing something else — a Passion Project Showcase. Whole grade. Whole auditorium. You bring something real, you show it.
Wait — like, ACTUALLY public? Parents-and-folding-chairs public?
Full auditorium. Whole grade invited. Refreshments, if the vending machine guy ever calls me back.
...Public.
lines.action: taps the desk edge, four times

lines.text: That wasn't excitement-public. That was the other kind.
Okay, if it's public, somebody needs to make sure it doesn't turn into open mic night. I'm thinking committee. I'm thinking me.
lines.action: already leaning toward Jordan, half-whisper

lines.text: Honestly? Iconic. Can I do the playlist. I've had one ready since September, I don't know why, I just had a feeling.
Wait — is this the kind of showcase where everyone claps politely, or the kind where somebody cries? Just — planning purposes.
Statistically? Both. Sign-ups by Friday. Let's make something worth showing up for.
lines.action: does a slow scan of the room, eyes land briefly on Alex

lines.text: ...Yeah. Worth showing.
lines.action: not looking up, mostly to self

lines.text: Okay — committee. Showcase committee. I'm putting it together, and I want you on it.
lines.action: catches Wren's arm, easy

lines.text: Committee for what, exactly? Please tell me it's not the clipboard kind.
It's a little bit the clipboard kind. It's also the 'decide what the whole grade sees' kind, which is objectively the fun kind.
Honestly, it's gonna be so fun, you'd basically be curating the whole— tastefully! Curating tastefully.
lines.action: hovering, hand almost landing on Wren's shoulder, glances at Maya mid-sentence

lines.text: We're just trimming a few things. Time's tight, not everything can go on. You know how it is.
lines.action: snaps fingers softly

lines.text: Trimming meaning cutting the boring stuff, or trimming meaning cutting the weird stuff?
Same thing, right?
Ha — okay. That's a joke. Mostly a joke.
So. You in?
lines.action: waiting

lines.text: Okay — magic act, fine. Dance solo, fine. Hip hop crew, fine— ...'Alex R-C. A world I made up. Notebook.'
lines.action: reading off a list, stops

lines.text: That's it. That's the whole description.

lines.emotion: laughs half a beat late, then stops himself
lines.action: stares at his tray

lines.text: I mean — 'a world I made up'? For a stage. What's he gonna do, narrate his diary for six minutes?
I say we cut it. Free up two minutes for the double dutch team — they actually rehearsed.
lines.action: pen ready

lines.text: I mean... it's kind of a nice idea though? The notebook thing?
lines.emotion: small, careful, checking if he's allowed to say that

lines.text: It's a nice idea for nobody watching. Different thing.
lines.action: looks at Wren

lines.text: Well?
You're doing the thing where you stand there deciding if it's weird to sit down. Just sit down.
lines.action: not looking up, notebook spread open — coastlines, invented alphabet, cramped handwriting crowding the margins

lines.text: ...Okay, this is either a country or a crime scene, and I have questions either way.
lines.action: sits, looking at the pages

lines.text: It's a country. Sort of. I've been making it since I was nine.
Nine. So this is what, four years of committed worldbuilding? That's not a hobby, Alex, that's a mortgage.
...That was actually good. I hate that I laughed.
I signed up. For the showcase. With this.
lines.action: taps the notebook edge, four times

lines.text: I don't know why I did that.
...Okay. And?
Do you think people are gonna laugh? Like — actually laugh. Not the nice kind. If I show them the real thing.
lines.action: finally looks up, direct, unguarded in a way that clearly costs something

lines.text: It stays on the list.
Didn't ask you to referee, Wren.
I mean — technically you kind of did. You're the one who put me on the committee that referees stuff.
...Whatever. It's one slot. Doesn't matter.
lines.action: twirls a braid, mouth tight

lines.text: Don't act surprised when people are confused, though.
Honestly? Kind of curious what's actually in the notebook now.
Great. Wonderful. Can we finish the list before lunch ends, or are we all just feelings today?
lines.action: snaps fingers, recovering fast

lines.text: Done. Moving on — hip hop crew, two minutes or three?
lines.action: crosses it off the list, easy
glances at Wren — not accusing, just noticing

lines.text: ...Three. Let's say three.
lines.action: looks away first, back to the list

lines.text: Nobody's going to ask why you didn't say anything. That's the worst part.
It's the best thing about you, honestly. You should show them the real one.
...Okay.
lines.action: stares at Wren like checking for a joke that isn't coming

lines.text: There's this part. Nobody's seen this part.
lines.action: flips to a deeper page, hands careful

lines.text: ...Is that a language? Alex, is that a whole alphabet?
It's how they write grief. There's a different one for good news. I never — I never finished that one.
lines.action: taps four times, softer now — almost excited instead of anxious

lines.text: I'm doing this one. The real one. If it's bad, it's bad. But it'll be mine.
Maybe... do something a little easier to explain? Keep the notebook part just for you.
...Yeah. No, that's — that's smart.

lines.action: closes the notebook a little too carefully

lines.text: I could do the volcano diorama thing. Everyone likes volcanoes. Volcanoes are safe.
That's not — I didn't mean —
It's fine. Efficient. Leaves more time for not talking about it.
Wait, why's everyone's phone— 'POV you peaked in third grade'— that's not — why would somebody—
lines.action: laughing before he means to, laugh dying mid-sentence, trails off, guilty

lines.text: Oh my— okay that's actually kind of—
lines.action: laughs on reflex, sees Wren's face, stops
staring at the tray, grip tightening on the fork

lines.text: ...Who even took a picture of that.
lines.action: quieter, glancing around — checking who's still laughing

lines.text: Okay — does anyone want a snack break? I have gum. I have a lot of gum.
A few heads turn toward Wren. Waiting. Half-expecting her to do something, since — she's friends with him, right?
Okay — can everyone put their phones down for like, one second?
lines.action: already standing before deciding to

lines.text: The cafeteria does that specific kind of quiet.
That's not yours to laugh at. It wasn't posted for you. Somebody stole it — that's the actual joke here. Not the notebook.
...She's right.
lines.action: quiet, to the table, not piling on

lines.text: ...You didn't have to do that.
Yeah, well. Somebody had to say it before Jordan tried to fix it with gum.
I DO have gum. Still offering. To anyone. Forever.
Hands shaking. Heart going too fast. Worth it. Probably worth it.
Hey. C'mon. Over here.
lines.action: pulling Alex toward the alcove, quiet, not making a scene
lets themselves be pulled, silent for a second

lines.text: ...This always happens somewhere. Different school, same joke, basically.
lines.action: taps four times on the wall

lines.text: This one's not a different school. This one's here. I'm not going anywhere.
...Okay.
Thanks for not making it a whole thing.
I make everything a whole thing. This is just a quiet whole thing.
...There it is.
lines.emotion: half-laugh, late, checking it's safe first

lines.text: The laughter keeps going. Wren doesn't move.
Say something. Say anything.
Across the cafeteria, Alex looks up — finds Wren's face. Checking.
Wren looks away first.
...Yeah. That's an answer too.
Hey — glad I caught you. I heard about today. Cafeteria telegraph's faster than the actual PA system.
lines.action: leaning against the desk

lines.text: Cool, cool — so that's not a rumor I need to worry about, right? No? Cool.
I'm not gonna make you relive it.
lines.action: taps two knuckles on the desk

lines.text: Here's the thing — I'm finalizing the showcase order, and I keep looking at Alex's slot.
I could move it. Smaller room, earlier day, less of an audience. Nobody has to know it was on purpose.
I used to beg teachers to move my name down the list too. Never once helped. Never stopped asking, though.
Can I ask you something. Don't answer yet — let me just... say it right.
lines.action: notebook clutched, tapping the cover fast, four at a time

lines.text: Do you actually want to be here? Or are you just being nice. You can tell me. I'd rather know.
Next up — Alex Rivera-Chen.
lines.action: at the small podium, warm

lines.text: Alex walks up holding the actual notebook. Not the backup. The real one.
So — um. This is a world. I made it. It's got a coastline, and a history, and a language for grief, and—
—and I never showed anyone the whole thing before today.
lines.emotion: voice steadying, finding Wren in the crowd

lines.text: Genuine applause. Maya, three rows back, nods once — like she means it.
Thanks for coming.
lines.emotion: easy, like it costs nothing to say — which means it costs everything

lines.text: Wouldn't have missed it.
Next up — Alex Rivera-Chen.
Alex walks up with a foam-board diorama. Competent. Careful. Safe.
This is a volcano. It erupts. That's — that's the project.
Polite applause. The kind that fills a room without meaning to.
Thanks for coming.
lines.emotion: light, but something behind it stays closed

lines.text: Of course.

options.text: Join the committee — you can always steer it from the inside.
Pass — you've got somewhere else to be. Go find Alex.

a.text: Knew you'd get it. First meeting's lunch, courtyard. Don't be weird about it.
This is gonna be great, honestly, no for real—

b.text: ...Your loss. Door's open if you change your mind. Door's not locked or whatever.
I'll, um. I'll tell her you said hi? To Alex. If that's — yeah.

options.text: Speak up — vouch for the project.
Let it go. It's not your fight.

a.text: ...Fine. Since you clearly have a whole speech ready.

options.text: "It's the best thing about you. Show them the real one."
"Maybe do something easier to explain — keep the notebook private."

a.emotion: checking for the catch and not finding one

options.text: Call it out — loudly, right now, in front of everyone.
Pull Alex aside quietly. Deal with the rest later.

a.text: Wren is already standing before she's decided to.

c.text: Somebody else will say something. Somebody else always— (nobody does.)

options.text: "Don't move anything yet — ask Alex what they actually want."
"Move them to the smaller slot. Less crowd, less risk."

a.text: ...Yeah. Yeah, that's fair. Should've thought of that first, honestly.

b.text: You sure that's what they want? Or just what's easier for the rest of us to watch?

options.text: Tell the truth — name the cost, and say you're choosing this anyway.
Crack a joke. Keep it light. Don't actually answer.

b.text: Alex looks back down at the notebook. Doesn't ask again.

lines.text: You never once told me to make it smaller. Every single time.
You never once made it easy to say no.
Good. Don't start now.
You know I almost cut it myself. After the list thing.
lines.action: pause

lines.text: After you didn't say anything.
lines.emotion: not an accusation anymore, just a fact

lines.text: I know. I'm sorry that happened before I fixed it.
You didn't have to fix it. You did anyway. That's the part I'm keeping.
You didn't have to sit through that. It wasn't even the real one.
lines.action: not quite looking at Wren

lines.text: ...I know.
Yeah. Cool.
lines.action: small, careful, done deciding something

lines.text: See you around, Wren.
Alex doesn't say 'notebook.' Doesn't say 'sometime.' The door doesn't slam. It just doesn't open again the same way.

voiceNotes: Wren's lines lead with a joke or a question almost every time she's uncertain — it's armor, not evasion, and the moments she DOESN'T do it (scene-3b, scene-8) should read as heavier for the omission. Alex speaks in short, guarded sentences until the notebook is involved, at which point vocabulary and sentence length expand — track this shift line-by-line as a barometer of trust. Maya's cruelty is never really about Alex; watch for the beat where her performance drops (twirling the braid, the too-quiet 'who even took a picture of that') — she's rattled by the leak too, just can't afford to show it in front of a full table. Jordan's jokes should land slightly wrong on purpose — that's the character, not a flaw in the writing. Alvarez should never give Wren the answer outright; his function is to make her aware she's being asked to speak for someone who can speak for themselves.

alternatives: Okay, seriously, phones down. One second.
Can we — hey. Actually stop for a second.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly less composed phrasing may better sell the 'standing before she decided to' stage direction — the line should sound like it's outrunning her nerve, not fully formed.

alternatives: That's not what I— never mind.
I didn't mean it like that, I just—
alternativeLines.reasoning: Wren trailing off completely (rather than a clean apology) fits the 'too late, already gone' beat better and avoids resolving the disappointment too neatly.

alternatives: Trimming like, cutting for time, or trimming like, cutting for cringe?
So trimming's a nice word for cutting the weird stuff, or...?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Both alternatives keep Wren's questions-as-shield pattern but vary the bluntness depending on how much the player is meant to already suspect Maya.
